trajectory_summary: Holland runs a competent verify pass and keeps the call moving, but the call ends without a booked appointment, a locked callback time, or a resolved cost objection. Donna's cost anxiety — the call's central tension — surfaces twice and is met with generic reassurance both times. The bill-collection deferral is handled with a loose 'send me a text' handoff and a self-disclosed follow-up risk from Holland at close. The call's energy is neither built nor leaked dramatically; it simply dissipates into an unstructured Monday callback that has no anchor.
outcome_dispute: The v1 outcome tag is 'unknown.' Based on the transcript, the correct tag is 'tentative_callback_unbooked' — there is a mutual Monday follow-up agreement but no appointment was set, no time was locked, and no button-up protocol was executed. This is not a booked sit. If this is a paid lead, this is a missed sit; lead type is unconfirmed — flag for metadata per schema context.lead_types_and_bill_collection_rules.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Objection inversion on cost anxiety — flip the bill, don't reassure
primary_coaching_focus.quote: I'm interested, but I know they're very expensive. So if if this is gonna be very expensive, you could just take me off the list.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Donna's cost anxiety is the call's central tension and it surfaces twice. Holland had the $300/month bill in hand both times and responded with generic reassurance instead of flipping the frame. This is the schema's objection_inversion move applied directly — 'what you can't afford is your current bill' — and it was available and unused on both touches. Until Holland internalizes the flip, cost-anxious customers will continue to get brochure responses that leave the anxiety unresolved.

key_moments.quote: Hey, Donna. This is Holland with Ion. I have a request you were looking into solar for your home. How are you doing today?
key_moments.stewart_read: Holland's intro omits both the form-fill reference and any legitimacy anchor. The schema's standard intro template calls for referencing the form submission ('the solar form you filled out online') to legitimize the call and reduce scam suspicion. Holland's phrasing — 'I have a request you were looking into solar' — is grammatically awkward and provides no trust signal. The Tesla intro variant was not applicable here, but the standard variant's legitimacy beat was skipped entirely. The schema's what_great_looks_like criterion is 'legitimacy established in first 30 seconds' — this intro does not meet it.

key_moments.quote: I'm interested, but I know they're very expensive. So if if this is gonna be very expensive, you could just take me off the list.
key_moments.stewart_read: This is the call's highest-leverage moment. Donna has just volunteered her bill ($300/month), her motivation (incentives), and her primary anxiety (cost) in a single breath. The schema's objection_inversion move is directly applicable: 'What you can't afford is your current bill.' Holland instead responds with a generic reassurance ('solar is the most affordable it's ever been') that addresses the objection on its own terms rather than flipping it. The $300/month anchor was already in hand — Holland had the tool and didn't use it.

key_moments.quote: I see that you you have some trees on the southern side of your home. Are all of those trees still there?
key_moments.stewart_read: Holland identifies trees on the southern exposure via satellite and probes whether they're still present. Donna confirms they are and that they're tall. This is a qualification risk — southern-side tall trees are a shading variable that could affect system viability or panel count. Holland notes it ('Okay') and moves on without probing further: no follow-up on canopy density, no mention of whether tree removal is planned, no flag for the designer. The probe was initiated correctly but not completed.

key_moments.quote: I remember it being very, very expensive.
key_moments.stewart_read: This is the second touch of Donna's cost anxiety pattern. She's not just raising a price objection — she's sharing a memory of being priced out of something she wanted. That's an emotional disclosure, not a negotiating position. Holland's response ('there's been lots of upgrades and technology and updates and whatnot') is accurate but impersonal. It addresses the factual content of the objection while missing the emotional register entirely. A brief acknowledgment of the frustration before pivoting to the technology update would have cost nothing and built trust.
